source_notice: An Ottoman scholar born in Crimea, head of the Hanafites in Cairo, master of al-Suyūṭī. His method weaves three threads: the linguistic sense (luġawī), the rational and theological sense (ʿaqlī), and the spiritual sense (ṣūfī). For each Name he also indicates the servant's share — that which, of the Name, may become a virtue and a path in the human being.

jamal.sens: The Names of gentleness, of nearness, of the love that bends down.

jalal.sens: The Names of greatness, of justice, of the awe that makes one draw back.

kamal.sens: The Names of absolute Unity, where Beauty and Majesty coincide.

1.sens_court: The supreme Name that gathers all the Names.
1.lugha: According to al-Kāfījī, this is the proper Name of the Essence (ism al-dhāt), reserved for Him alone; no being has ever borne it, in the past or in the future. The grammarians differ: for some (الأُنْس) it is derived from al-ilāh (الإِلَه) (the worshipped), for others it is underived — a pure Name of the Essence.
1.aqli: It gathers all the attributes of perfection. Whoever says “Allāh” has, without knowing it, named all the beauties and all the majesties at once.
1.sufi: The invocation of this Name, says al-Kāfījī, opens the door; the other Names are its rays. The heart that takes root in it has no other dwelling.
1.part: Let the servant pronounce this Name only with reverence, and let his life become silent worship.

2.sens_court: The Mercy that precedes all things and embraces the entire universe.
2.lugha: An intensive form (faʿlān) on the root r-ḥ-m: the womb, the matrix. Al-Kāfījī specifies that this form indicates amplitude — a mercy so vast that it overflows onto every existent thing, believer or not.
2.aqli: This is the mercy of creation: it is through it that things are, rather than not being. The gift of existence before any other gift.
2.sufi: For al-Kāfījī, ar-Raḥmān is the breath (nafas (النَّفَس)) that sustains the worlds. At every instant, His mercy begins the world anew. Without it, the universe would return to nothingness.
2.part: Let the servant be merciful toward every being, without distinction — for the rain of ar-Raḥmān falls upon all.

3.sens_court: The Mercy that chooses, that bends down over the soul turned toward Him.
3.lugha: A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form, from the same root as ar-Raḥmān (اَلرَّحْمَٰن), but of more intimate reach. Al-Kāfījī says: ar-Raḥmān is for this world, ar-Raḥīm is for the next — the one is universal, the other elects.
3.aqli: This is the mercy of the return: it welcomes the penitent, soothes the burned, forgives the broken.
3.sufi: Where ar-Raḥmān gives being, ar-Raḥīm gives felicity. It is the smile of God upon the face of the faithful.
3.part: Let the servant, after having given, accompany — let him not be content with the general gift, but bend down over the details of another's pain.

4.sens_court: The absolute Sovereign, whose Kingdom is without bounds.
4.lugha: From m-l-k: to possess, to reign. Al-Kāfījī distinguishes al-Mālik (the Possessor) and al-Malik (the King who governs): God is both, but this Name underlines effective rule.
4.aqli: All that exists is His property and His subject. Not an atom moves without His permission.
4.sufi: The servant who contemplates this Name understands that he possesses nothing — not even his own breath. This understanding is the beginning of spiritual poverty (faqr (الفَقْر)).
4.part: Reign over your own inner kingdom: let reason govern the passions, and let the heart govern reason.

5.sens_court: Pure of all imperfection, transcending all that the mind can conceive.
5.lugha: An intensive form of q-d-s: to be pure, set apart, sanctified. Al-Kāfījī: “He is so holy that every description diminishes Him.”
5.aqli: No analogy reaches Him, no flaw touches Him. He is beyond the like and the contrary.
5.sufi: Al-Quddūs is the Name that purifies the heart of its inner idols — including the images one forms of God Himself.
5.part: Purify your heart, not only of fault, but also of the concepts that claim to enclose the Real.

6.sens_court: Peace in itself, the source of all peace.
6.lugha: From s-l-m: to be sound, intact, at peace. Al-Kāfījī: “He is Peace, for no alteration reaches Him, and from Him flows the peace of hearts.”
6.aqli: Being free of all lack, He is absolute security; creatures have peace only in proportion to their return toward Him.
6.sufi: The sakīna that descends upon the heart of the one who remembers is a reflection of this Name. Whoever enters into peace with God enters into peace with all.
6.part: Be a source of peace for whoever draws near you: let your presence calm without need of words.

7.sens_court: He who reassures, who makes faith secure.
7.lugha: From a-m-n: safety, trust. An active form: the One who grants safety (amān (أَمَان)).
7.aqli: He verifies His own word — confirms His promises, attests His truth through the signs of the world and of the soul.
7.sufi: The heart that rests in this Name no longer trembles before the vicissitudes: it knows that the promise holds.
7.part: Be reliable: let your word be a shelter, and not a trap.

8.sens_court: He who watches over all things, witness and preserver.
8.lugha: A rare word; al-Kāfījī recalls several etymologies: from amīn (أَمِين) (trustworthy), or from haymana (to spread one's wing over).
8.aqli: He observes, attests, preserves: nothing escapes His guard.
8.sufi: To feel this Name is to live under the gaze — not a gaze that surveils, but a gaze that broods over.
8.part: Watch over what is entrusted to you: your loved ones, your body, the passing of time.

9.sens_court: He who cannot be vanquished, whose strength is unreachable.
9.lugha: From ʿ-z-z: to be rare, strong, beyond reach.
9.aqli: His power is limited by nothing; no one has the slightest power over Him.
9.sufi: The servant who attaches himself to al-ʿAzīz becomes unreachable by the humiliations that come from outside: his centre is elsewhere.
9.part: Keep your dignity in poverty as in abundance; offer your heart only to what deserves it.

10.sens_court: He who repairs what is broken, and imposes what He wills.
10.lugha: From j-b-r; a twofold sense — jabara means both to set a broken bone and to compel. Al-Kāfījī insists on the first: He is above all the Restorer.
10.aqli: He restores broken hearts and straightens what leans; and no one compels His will.
10.sufi: When the soul is in pieces, this Name is the balm. Ibn ʿArabī would say: al-Jabbār does not diminish the broken — He recomposes it vaster than before.
10.part: Do not break what is already fragile; be jābir — a restorer — for those around you.

11.sens_court: The only One to whom greatness rightfully belongs.
11.lugha: A reflexive form of k-b-r: to manifest oneself as great. In the creature this is a flaw; in God it is a truth.
11.aqli: He is great in Himself, and not by comparison.
11.sufi: The more the servant effaces himself, the more this Name appears in clarity. Human pride is a theft committed against the only One who possesses greatness by full right.
11.part: Acknowledge that any greatness you feel within you has been lent to you — and give it back through gratitude.

12.sens_court: He who measures and makes be.
12.lugha: From kh-l-q: to measure with exactness before producing. Creation is first of all proportion.
12.aqli: He has created each thing according to its just measure (qadar (القَدَر)), neither too much nor too little.
12.sufi: To contemplate this Name in a blade of grass or a face: creation is not a past fact, it renews itself at every instant.
12.part: Create to your own measure: a gesture, a work, a word — but with exactness.

13.sens_court: He who distinguishes each being, draws it apart.
13.lugha: From b-r-ʾ: to separate, to distinguish from nothingness. Where al-Khāliq (اَلْخَالِق) conceives, al-Bāriʾ distinguishes.
13.aqli: No being is confused with another: each soul is unique within creation.
13.sufi: Your face never existed before you and will never exist again: it is a signature of al-Bāriʾ.
13.part: Honour the singularity of others — do not reduce them to a category.

14.sens_court: He who gives form and face.
14.lugha: From ṣ-w-r: form, image. An intensive form: the One who shapes with art.
14.aqli: He gives each being its own figure, never repeating.
14.sufi: The forms of the world are calligraphies of His Names. To learn to read forms is to learn to read God.
14.part: Give your life a beautiful form: let beauty too be a spiritual act.

15.sens_court: He who covers faults and effaces them without ceasing.
15.lugha: From gh-f-r: to veil, to cover with a helmet. An intensive form: He veils again and again.
15.aqli: No fault is too great for His forgiveness, provided one returns.
15.sufi: God's forgiveness precedes the servant's repentance: it is because He already forgives that the servant dares to return.
15.part: Forgive before you are asked; cover the weakness of others as you would wish your own to be covered.

16.sens_court: He before whom every force bends.
16.lugha: An intensive form of q-h-r: to subdue, to tame, to vanquish absolutely. Al-Kāfījī specifies that qahr is stronger than ghalaba: it is not only to defeat, it is to reduce to total powerlessness.
16.aqli: Every will other than His breaks against His will. The mighty of the earth grow old and die — proof that above them watches a Subduer.
16.sufi: Al-Qahhār is the Name that tames the inner passions. The spiritual combatant is not the one who destroys his outward enemies, but the one who takes his carnal soul captive.
16.part: Subdue your ego before it subdues you; and do not forget that you yourself will one day be tamed by one greater than you.

17.sens_court: He who gives without recompense or motive.
17.lugha: From w-h-b; hiba is the pure gift — without exchange, without debt. An intensive form: He gives and gives again, without wearying.
17.aqli: The gift precedes merit: before you existed, you had nothing that would justify being given being. Everything since is of this order.
17.sufi: To recognise this Name is to cease believing that one has earned anything. Gratitude is no longer mere courtesy: it becomes vertigo.
17.part: Give without expecting — neither return, nor recognition, nor even to see yourself give.

18.lugha: From r-z-q; rizq denotes all that sustains — bread, breath, knowledge, consolation. An intensive form: subsistence is continual.
18.aqli: Not a mouth is opened on earth for which He has not prepared its food. The ant beneath the stone is nourished just as the king is.
18.sufi: There is a rizq of the body and a rizq of the heart. The second is rarer and more necessary: wisdom, presence, right encounters.
18.part: Work for your subsistence, but without anguish; and be yourself nourishment for others — through your word, your time, your attention.

19.sens_court: He who opens the ways and the hearts.
19.lugha: From f-t-ḥ: to open, to conquer, to unveil. An intensive form: He ceaselessly opens what seemed shut.
19.aqli: He opens the doors of the world (subsistence, ways, encounters) and the doors of the soul (understanding, repentance, unveiling).
19.sufi: The fatḥ is, in the Sufi way, spiritual opening — the moment when a truth long approached suddenly opens. No one can bring it about; one only makes oneself openable.
19.part: Open what you can open: a closed door, a shut heart, a broken dialogue — and leave to God what you cannot open.

20.sens_court: Whose knowledge embraces all things, manifest and secret.
20.lugha: A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form of ʿ-l-m: to know with certainty. Al-Kāfījī notes: His knowledge is not acquired, it is essential — He knows by Himself.
20.aqli: His knowledge has neither before nor after: He knows the past without memory, the future without foresight, the present without observation.
20.sufi: To live under the gaze of al-ʿAlīm is to cease playing a role. There is no use in seeming: He sees even beneath the veil you lay over yourself.
20.part: Seek true knowledge — not the erudition that puffs up, but the knowing that transforms.

21.sens_court: Who restricts, withdraws, makes one feel the contraction.
21.lugha: From q-b-ḍ: to seize, to close the hand, to contract. The gesture of the hand that shuts.
21.aqli: He contracts subsistence, health, joy — according to a wisdom we do not measure. At the threshold of death, it is still He who gathers the soul.
21.sufi: Al-Qabḍ is also a spiritual state: the contraction of the heart, dryness, inner narrowness. It is not a chastisement but a work — the flower shuts at night to open in the morning.
21.part: Accept your contractions without panic: the hand that closes will open again.

22.sens_court: Who enlarges the heart and extends grace.
22.lugha: From b-s-ṭ: to extend, to unfold, to open the hand. The gesture inverse to qabḍ (قَبْض).
22.aqli: He extends subsistence, health, the horizon. He enlarges the narrow heart until it becomes vaster than the world.
22.sufi: Al-Basṭ is the state in which the heart breathes widely, without care. It is the gift of the moment, not an acquisition — to alternate qabḍ and basṭ is the normal breathing of the wayfarer.
22.part: When expansion comes, do not cling to it; when it departs, do not despair. Acknowledge that both hands are His.

23.lugha: From kh-f-ḍ; literally: to make descend, to incline downward.
23.aqli: He lowers unjust powers, empty renowns, empires that believe themselves eternal. History is the great narrative of this Name.
23.sufi: But He also lowers in order to raise: the buried grain descends before it rises. Every humiliation accepted prepares an elevation.
23.part: Lower yourself of your own accord: spare God from having to do it.

24.lugha: From r-f-ʿ: to lift, to hoist, to raise up.
24.aqli: He raises the humble, the sincere, the unseen. Many a saint has had no other elevation than this — a name unknown to men, written in letters of gold with Him.
24.sufi: True elevation has nothing to do with social rank: it is nearness to God, which often passes by way of the stripping-away of rank.
24.part: Do not seek to rise: seek to be just, and leave elevation to His hand.

25.sens_court: Who grants dignity to whom He wills.
25.lugha: From ʿ-z-z; a causative form: to make ʿazīz — rare, precious, untouchable.
25.aqli: True honour comes neither from birth nor from wealth, but from Him alone. Whomever He honours, no one can shame.
25.sufi: The spiritual dignity (ʿizza) of the servant grows as he depends less on creatures. The more he is in need, the less he is free.
25.part: Keep your dignity through poverty toward creatures and richness in God.

26.sens_court: Who strips glory from the one who arrays himself in it.
26.lugha: From dh-l-l; a causative form: to make dhalīl — base, brought low.
26.aqli: He humbles those who swell with a dignity that is not their own. The proud one fashions his own downfall.
26.sufi: Humility accepted before God protects from the humiliation inflicted by men. Whoever bows of his own will need no longer be bowed down.
26.part: Never place your glory in what can be taken from you.

27.sens_court: No voice, no murmur escapes Him.
27.lugha: A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form of s-m-ʿ; al-Kāfījī insists: He hears the murmur of the ant upon the black rock in the dark of night.
27.aqli: His hearing is not an organ: it is a presence to every vibration of being. Silence too is heard.
27.sufi: When the heart is heavy and the tongue falls silent, know that He already hears. The highest prayer is sometimes the one we cannot put into words.
27.part: Be attentive: listen before you speak, listen more than you speak, listen to what is not said.

28.sens_court: His sight passes through the veils.
28.lugha: A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form of b-ṣ-r; baṣīra (بَصِيرَة) is penetrating vision, which sees the invisible behind the visible.
28.aqli: He sees the thought before the word, the intention before the gesture. No veil is opaque to Him — not even the veil of the self over itself.
28.sufi: Al-Baṣīr grants His servants a share of baṣīra: the lucidity that blinds itself neither about others nor about oneself.
28.part: Ask for baṣīra more than for sight: to see clearly is worth more than to see far.

29.lugha: From ḥ-k-m: to judge, to settle, to seal. A faʿal form that names the function itself: the Judge.
29.aqli: No dispute remains without a verdict: what is not settled here below will be settled there, with a justice that does not err.
29.sufi: To cease being a judge of others is to entrust oneself to al-Ḥakam: He knows what you do not know of hearts.
29.part: Do not rush your judgement upon another; leave it to a Judge greater than you.

30.lugha: From ʿ-d-l: to hold in balance, to set straight. Here the word denotes not an attribute but the very essence of justice.
30.aqli: He is so just that one says not that He acts justly, but that He is Justice. The apparent evil of the world, over the whole span of time, resolves into equity.
30.sufi: When you suffer an injustice, do not tense up: the balance of al-ʿAdl weighs at this very instant what your eyes do not see.
30.part: Be just — first toward yourself: do not betray yourself, do not idolise yourself. Then toward others, even toward the enemy.

31.sens_court: His gentleness acts where no one suspects it.
31.lugha: From l-ṭ-f; luṭf unites two senses: fineness (that which is imperceptible) and kindness (that which softens itself in order to reach).
31.aqli: His knowledge penetrates to the most deeply buried secrets, and His action insinuates itself into things with a delicacy that crushes nothing.
31.sufi: Al-Laṭīf is the Name of the invisible graces: those coincidences that save, those words that arrive just in time, those consolations that come without a sound. Often you recognise their work only afterward.
31.part: Be gentle in your manners, fine in your attentions; enter another's life without trampling it.

32.sens_court: Knower of secrets and of nuances.
32.lugha: From kh-b-r; khabar is news, true information. A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form: He knows through intimate experience, not by hearsay.
32.aqli: His knowledge descends to the detail: the movement of an atom, the nuance of an intention, the weight of a silence.
32.sufi: Where al-ʿAlīm (اَلْعَلِيم) knows things, al-Khabīr knows their savour, their background, their hidden underside.
32.part: Seek to know things in depth, not at the surface; and acknowledge your own opacity to yourself.

33.sens_court: Who is patient, not hasty to punish.
33.lugha: From ḥ-l-m; ḥilm (حِلْم) is self-mastery before what irritates, the absence of haste to react.
33.aqli: He defers the chastisement, grants the guilty time, opens the door of return until the last breath.
33.sufi: How many of us, had we been punished on the spot for every fault, would no longer be here to repent of it? Ḥilm is the great reprieve that makes the way possible.
33.part: Learn to defer your reaction; between the offence and the response, leave a space — it is there that your freedom dwells.

34.lugha: From ʿ-ẓ-m; ʿaẓama is greatness in majesty, the kind that inspires respect and trembling.
34.aqli: All created greatness is small beside His; and all created greatnesses added together do not form even one atom of His.
34.sufi: To meditate on this Name restores to their just measure the great cares that occupy the soul: beside al-ʿAẓīm, what seemed enormous shrinks.
34.part: Keep a sense of proportion: do not make much of what is small, nor little of what is great.

35.sens_court: Whose forgiveness covers the deepest faults.
35.lugha: The same root as al-Ghaffār (اَلْغَفَّار) (gh-f-r: to veil), but a faʿūl (فَعُول) form that underlines the depth of the forgiveness rather than its repetition.
35.aqli: No sin is too old, no fault too black, not to be covered by His forgiveness, provided the heart returns.
35.sufi: Al-Ghaffār repeats, al-Ghafūr penetrates: He is not content to efface, He transforms the scar itself into light.
35.part: When you forgive, go to the very bottom: a surface forgiveness reopens.

36.sens_court: Who multiplies in return the little that one offers.
36.lugha: From sh-k-r: to acknowledge a benefit. Strange: a grateful God? Al-Kāfījī explains: it is because He rewards beyond the measure of the gesture — He multiplies the grain of good that is sown.
36.aqli: An atom of kindness on your part meets a mountain of reward on His. Such is His gratitude.
36.sufi: To understand this Name heals one of bitterness: you are never forgotten, never undervalued. The good you do is known, even when men ignore it.
36.part: Be grateful for the least benefit received, and acknowledge the least good you receive from others — gratitude is an art that is cultivated.

37.lugha: From ʿ-l-w; ʿuluww is elevation. A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form: He is in essential height, not spatial.
37.aqli: His elevation is not in a geographical above: it is qualitative — beyond analogy, beyond the more than.
37.sufi: To lift one's eyes toward Him is not to look upward: it is to open another eye, which sees neither high nor low, but the Real.
37.part: Raise your intentions: do not ask little of a Most High.

38.lugha: From k-b-r; kibr (الكِبْر) is greatness; kabīr speaks essential greatness, not comparative.
38.aqli: All created greatness holds its greatness from Him. This is why the ritual invocation Allāhu akbar (اَللَّٰهُ أَكْبَر) does not mean “God is greater” — but “God is the Greatness.”
38.sufi: When you say Allāhu akbar on entering prayer, you lay down at the door all the false greatnesses: your own, that of men, that of your cares.
38.part: Do not make yourself great; there is only one Great.

39.sens_court: Who guards and conserves all things.
39.lugha: From ḥ-f-ẓ; ḥifẓ is the keeping — the kind one has for what is precious. The ḥifẓ of the Quran is its memorisation: one keeps it within oneself.
39.aqli: He preserves the existent in being at every instant. He also keeps the deed — not one act is lost in His memory.
39.sufi: Your story is not lost: all the good you have done, all that you have suffered unjustly, is kept. Nothing evaporates.
39.part: Be a keeper: of your word, of your promises, of the dignity of others, of the things entrusted to you.

40.sens_court: Who gives each one its just nourishment.
40.lugha: From q-w-t; qūt (قُوت) is precise, measured nourishment, that which suffices. More targeted than rizq.
40.aqli: To each being, the exact portion that keeps it alive — neither too much nor too little. The knowledge of this measure escapes creatures entirely.
40.sufi: There is a spiritual qūt: the measure of knowledge, of suffering, of joy that your soul needs today. You receive exactly what keeps you alive — no more, no less.
40.part: Be temperate in consumption, generous in sharing. Give others their just portion.

41.sens_court: He suffices for the one who leans upon Him.
41.lugha: From ḥ-s-b; a twofold sense: to reckon (He keeps the exact account) and to suffice (He alone is a sufficient reckoning).
41.aqli: For the one for whom He suffices, nothing more is lacking. For the one for whom He does not suffice, nothing will ever suffice.
41.sufi: Ḥasbunā Allāh (اَللَّٰه) wa niʿma-l-Wakīl (اَلْوَكِيل) — “God suffices us, and what an excellent guardian”: the formula of moments of trial, when every support gives way save that one.
41.part: Learn to say this is enough for me. It is the beginning of freedom.

42.sens_court: Of a majesty that inspires awe.
42.lugha: From j-l-l; jalāl (جَلَال) is the greatness that overawes, that makes one draw back. A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form: He is essentially majestic.
42.aqli: His majesty inspires an awe-struck stillness (hayba (الهَيْبَة)) that is not fear, but a reverence that effaces in us the idle chatter.
42.sufi: Before al-Jalīl, the heart ceases to argue, to propose, to calculate. It simply stands there.
42.part: Learn to stand in silence before what exceeds you. Do not profane with words what deserves recollectedness.

43.sens_court: Who gives without recalling His gifts.
43.lugha: From k-r-m; karam unites material generosity and inner nobility. The karīm gives, and he gives well — without humiliating, without reminding.
43.aqli: He gives before being asked, gives what one would not have known to ask for, gives above merit.
43.sufi: Al-Karīm does not make the one who receives feel that he receives. The best generosity is the one we forget having shown — as He does.
43.part: Give with a hand that knows not what the other gives; and forget at once what you have given.

44.sens_court: Present to all, an attentive witness.
44.lugha: From r-q-b; raqaba is the nape of the neck — hence to watch over: the one who stands behind you, who sees the back of your neck.
44.aqli: No act, no thought unfolds without His attentive presence.
44.sufi: To live in the sense of ar-Raqīb is murāqaba — inner vigilance: making of each act an act done before Him, hence in full presence.
44.part: Watch over yourself as you would watch over a child: not to punish, but so as not to lose sight of it.

45.lugha: From j-w-b; jawāb is the answer. An active form: He answers in act.
45.aqli: He answers the call — sometimes by giving what is asked, sometimes by giving better, sometimes by deferring for a greater gift.
45.sufi: Do not judge His answer by the immediate yes or no alone: the prayer not granted here sometimes receives its answer at another time, or in an invisible transformation of yourself.
45.part: Answer those who call you: leave no cry without an echo, to the measure of your power.

46.sens_court: His mercy and His knowledge embrace all things.
46.lugha: From w-s-ʿ; saʿa is amplitude, extent — the opposite of narrowness (ḍīq).
46.aqli: The whole universe does not fill His amplitude; human miseries added together do not exhaust His mercy.
46.sufi: Narrowness outside, but amplitude within: the servant of al-Wāsiʿ knows that the inner space has no walls.
46.part: Enlarge your heart: do not let it shrink to the dimensions of your cares.

47.sens_court: Who sets each thing in its just place.
47.lugha: From ḥ-k-m (the same root as al-Ḥakam (اَلْحَكَم)); ḥikma (حِكْمَة) is wisdom — knowing and acting rightly. A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form: He is Wise by essence.
47.aqli: Not an atom of His work is in surplus, not a detail is wanting. What we do not understand bears witness to this as much as what we do understand.
47.sufi: When the meaning of a trial escapes you, recall: it is you who lack height, not He who lacks wisdom.
47.part: Seek wisdom more than knowledge; act with measure more than by impulse.

48.sens_court: Who loves and lets Himself be loved.
48.lugha: From w-d-d; wudd is love-tenderness, lasting affection. A faʿūl (فَعُول) form that can be active (He loves) and passive (He is loved).
48.aqli: He loves His creatures before they love Him; it is His love that calls forth theirs in return.
48.sufi: Al-Wadūd is the secret Name of the Sufis: God is not only to be feared, He is to be loved. The whole way lies in this shift.
48.part: Love, simply. Love those who love you, love those who do not love you. And know that, first of all, you are loved.

49.sens_court: Of a glory full of goodness.
49.lugha: From m-j-d; majd combines greatness and generosity — the glory that pours itself out, not the one that keeps itself.
49.aqli: His glory is not haughty: it manifests in the gift, the radiance, the beauty.
49.sufi: He is glorious in His very mercy: majesty casts no shadow over beauty, it crowns it.
49.part: Let your dignity never be dry: let it be inhabited by generosity.

50.sens_court: Who raises up from among the dead.
50.lugha: From b-ʿ-th; baʿth is the sending, and more precisely the raising-up — making rise what is lying down.
50.aqli: He will raise the dead on the promised Day; but already He raises each morning, from the sleep that resembles death, and from deeper sleeps still.
50.sufi: Every spiritual awakening is a small resurrection: a sleeping heart rises again, a hope thought dead breathes anew.
50.part: Raise up what has fallen: a discouraged loved one, an abandoned work, a friendship grown cold.

51.sens_court: Present to every being at every instant.
51.lugha: From sh-h-d: to be present, to see, to attest. Shahāda is at once presence and testimony.
51.aqli: He is present to all things and attests to all things: not an outside witness, but a presence inward to the event.
51.sufi: Ash-Shahīd is the great antidote to solitude: you have never acted alone, never suffered alone, never loved alone. You have always been seen.
51.part: Live as though someone saw you — for it is so. And bear witness to the true, even against yourself.

52.sens_court: Reality itself, set against all that vanishes.
52.lugha: From ḥ-q-q; ḥaqq (حَقّ) is at once truth (against the false), reality (against illusion), and right (against the unjust).
52.aqli: All that is not He has only a borrowed being; at every instant He alone is, and makes be.
52.sufi: Al-Ḥaqq is the most spoken Name of the Sufis. Anā-l-Ḥaqq (أَنَا الْحَقّ); Yā Ḥaqq — not because they identify themselves with God, but because all the rest suddenly appears to them as less real than He.
52.part: Seek the Real beneath the appearance; do not lie, and do not accept the lie within yourself.

53.sens_court: He to whom one entrusts one's affairs.
53.lugha: From w-k-l; the wakīl is the one to whom a matter is handed over so that he may carry it through. A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form: He is essentially worthy of this handing-over.
53.aqli: To Him all affairs may be entrusted: material, moral, spiritual. He loses nothing of what is handed to Him.
53.sufi: Tawakkul derives from this Name: not passivity, but the gesture of handing over after having done one's part. You do what you must; the outcome, you lay down.
53.part: Learn to let go of results. You are not the director of the world — and that is good news.

54.lugha: From q-w-y; quwwa is strength, the capacity to act. A faʿīl (فَعِيل) form: He is Strength in essence, not by accumulation.
54.aqli: His strength does not tire, does not wear out, meets no equal. It needs no exercise in order to exist.
54.sufi: The servant who leans on al-Qawī receives, within weakness, a strength that is not his own: it passes through the trial by him, more than by his own reserves.
54.part: Take no vanity in your strengths — they are lent. And feel no shame at your weaknesses — they make room for one stronger than you.

55.lugha: From m-t-n; matn is firmness, the consistency of a tightly woven fabric. More precise than al-Qawī (اَلْقَوِيّ): the strength that does not give way.
55.aqli: No fatigue eats into His power; no repetition wears it; no century diminishes it.
55.sufi: In holding to Him, you hold to what does not tremble. The rest — opinions, fashions, moods — will pass.
55.part: Seek the firm within yourself: that in you which can withstand the trial without collapsing.

56.sens_court: Friend and protector of His own.
56.lugha: From w-l-y; walāya (وَلَايَة) is protecting nearness, faithful alliance. The walī (وَلِيّ) is at once friend, ally, guardian.
56.aqli: He is the ally of His sincere servants: He protects them, guides them, takes their affair in hand.
56.sufi: From this Name derives the term walī in the sense of saint: the one who has become an intimate of God, and whom God takes under His particular wing. Sainthood is less a performance than a friendship.
56.part: Choose your allies with care; and be a faithful friend to those who have trusted you.

57.sens_court: Praised for Himself, independently of all praise.
57.lugha: From ḥ-m-d; ḥamd is praise — not only shukr (الشُّكْر) (thanks for a gift received), but the acknowledgement of excellence in itself.
57.aqli: He is praised whether or not He is praised. The whole of creation, by its very existence, is a silent praise.
57.sufi: Al-ḥamdu li-Llāh: praise belongs to God, in every situation. To say it in joy is easy; to say it in trial — that is the way.
57.part: Praise before you ask; acknowledge the good before the lack.

58.lugha: From ḥ-ṣ-y; iḥṣāʾ is the exact reckoning. An active form: He numbers in the present.
58.aqli: All is counted with Him: the grains of sand, the breaths, the drops of rain, the hidden good deeds, the secret faults.
58.sufi: Nothing invisible has been lost: not your unnoticed pain, not your unremarked patience, not your forgotten kindness. All is in His great book.
58.part: Do not do good so that it may be counted by men — it is already counted elsewhere.

59.lugha: From b-d-ʾ: to begin. An active form: the One who inaugurates, who makes begin.
59.aqli: Every thing that begins to exist, He is its first author. He does not draw His own beginning from another.
59.sufi: At the beginning of every right thought, of every impulse toward the good, there is He — the first cause, with no cause behind it.
59.part: Acknowledge that you are the origin of nothing. The ideas that come to you, the strengths that carry you — all have a source upstream of you.

60.sens_court: Who makes return to the point of origin.
60.lugha: From ʿ-w-d; ʿaud is the return, the resumption. An active form: He makes return.
60.aqli: All that He has begun will return to Him: innā li-Llāhi wa innā ilayhi rājiʿūn — “We belong to God, and it is to Him that we return.”
60.sufi: The whole way lies in this verb: to return. The servant creates nothing, he returns. And at the end, it is still al-Muʿīd who brings him back, more than his own steps.
60.part: Know where you are going: not forward, but toward the origin. The goal is also the source.

61.lugha: From ḥ-y-y; ḥayāt (حَيَاة), life. A causative form: He makes alive.
61.aqli: Life in all its forms — biological, mental, spiritual — flows from Him at every instant. The dead earth grows green as a sign: so too will hearts.
61.sufi: Al-Muḥyī revives dead hearts: there is a life above biological life, and it is this that the way seeks.
61.part: Give life around you: through your words that encourage, your attention that awakens, your presence that revives.

62.lugha: From m-w-t; mawt, death. A causative form: He makes die.
62.aqli: No creature dies without His decree. Death is not a failure of the system: it is part of it, by His ordinance.
62.sufi: There is a death before death — death to the ego, fanāʾ (فَنَاء). Al-Mumīt is also the Name of this inner death that opens another life.
62.part: Learn to die before dying: each day, to each false image of yourself, to each attachment that divides you. You will then die more freely.

63.lugha: From ḥ-y-y; ḥayy, the Living — not living from something, but Living in essence.
63.aqli: His life is not a state, it is His very being. All life is a reflection, never a copy.
63.sufi: Al-Ḥayy is one of the two Names of the Verse of the Throne (Āyat al-Kursī (آيَةُ الكُرْسِيّ)), with al-Qayyūm (اَلْقَيُّوم). To invoke them together is to take root in what does not die.
63.part: Do not place your life in what dies. And within what dies, seek the living.

64.sens_court: Who stands by Himself and makes all the rest stand.
64.lugha: From q-w-m; qiyām is to stand upright, to subsist. An intensive form: He subsists by Himself, and all the rest subsists by Him.
64.aqli: If His qayyūmiyya were interrupted for an instant, the universe would cease to be. The keeping-in-being is as miraculous as the bringing-into-being.
64.sufi: Yā Ḥayy (اَلْحَيّ) yā Qayyūm is the invocation of moments of exhaustion: You who are living and who stand upright, hold me upright.
64.part: Stand upright. And hold upright what threatens to fall around you.

65.lugha: From w-j-d; wajd is to find — including in the existential sense, wujūd being being. The wājid is also the one who lacks nothing: He has all within Himself.
65.aqli: He never seeks — He has all. No resource fails Him, no means is wanting to Him.
65.sufi: Among the Sufis, wajd also denotes ecstasy — the inner finding. The servant finds God: not as an object, but as a sudden self-evidence.
65.part: Be, in that poverty, rich: lack not the essential, and the essential is already within you.

66.sens_court: Of a nobility full of generosity.
66.lugha: An active form of the root m-j-d (the same as al-Majīd (اَلْمَجِيد)); the nuance: al-Mājid stresses the present act of nobility, al-Majīd the essential attribute.
66.aqli: He is noble in the instant as in eternity: His acting and His being never contradict each other.
66.sufi: The nobility of the soul is from Him: futuwwa (الفُتُوَّة) (spiritual chivalry) draws its source from this Name.
66.part: Be noble: not haughty, but great of heart. True nobility is measured by what one forgives, not by what one demands.

67.lugha: From w-ḥ-d; waḥda, unity. Wāḥid: the One who excludes multiplicity within His essence.
67.aqli: He has neither partner, nor part, nor like, nor equivalent. To think more than one is to step outside Him.
67.sufi: Tawḥīd, the profession of unity, is the heart of the way: not a dogma to recite, but an experience to live — to see that nothing else has an existence of its own.
67.part: Unify your heart: do not serve two masters, do not run in two directions, do not wear two faces.

68.lugha: From the same root as al-Wāḥid (اَلْوَاحِد), but more radical: aḥad excludes not only multiplicity, but all internal composition.
68.aqli: Where al-Wāḥid says sole, al-Aḥad says simple — uncompounded, undivisible, with neither inside nor outside.
68.sufi: It is by this Name that sūrat al-Ikhlāṣ opens: Qul huwa-Llāhu Aḥad. Uniqueness is there stated in its pure state.
68.part: Be simple: a single heart, a single intention, a single aim.

69.sens_court: Full, without hollow, toward whom all turns.
69.lugha: A rare word, with a twofold sense: without hollow or flaw — hence full, self-sufficient; and the one toward whom one turns in need.
69.aqli: He needs nothing, and all things need Him. He has neither hunger, nor thirst, nor sleep, nor lack of any kind.
69.sufi: Aṣ-Ṣamad is the Name of the ultimate Refuge: when all the doors are shut, one turns toward the One who has no door, because He is wholly welcome.
69.part: Turn toward Him in need — first. And learn, in your turn, to be a refuge for whoever turns toward you.

70.sens_court: Who can do all that He wills.
70.lugha: From q-d-r; qudra is the capacity to act. Qādir, an active form: the One who can.
70.aqli: His power is without limit: neither resistance that obstructs Him, nor effort that costs Him.
70.sufi: To feel al-Qādir is to cease despairing: what is humanly impossible is not so for Him.
70.part: Do not say it is impossible too soon. The door will not open of itself — but it can open.

71.sens_court: Whose power is exercised without effort.
71.lugha: An augmented form of the same root; iqtidār adds to qudra the notion of effective mastery and of ease.
71.aqli: What He wills is done, without a necessary intermediary, without gradation, without difficulty.
71.sufi: Where al-Qādir (اَلْقَادِر) says He can, al-Muqtadir says He can absolutely, and without effort. It is the Name of the great reversals — when the oppressor falls in an instant.
71.part: Do not fear the mighty; know that their power is borrowed, and that the reversal is in His hand.

72.sens_court: Who raises what He wills to the first rank.
72.lugha: From q-d-m; qadima is to precede, to be at the head. An intensive form: He places forward.
72.aqli: He places first what He wills: the prophets, the saints, the just works. And He places first, in each one's life, what ought to be.
72.sufi: Inner order matters: what you put first in your life reveals who you are. God first, and the rest follows in the just hierarchy.
72.part: Put first what ought to be first; inner disorder always arises from a wrong priority.

73.sens_court: Who keeps what He wills behind.
73.lugha: From ʾ-kh-r; taʾakhkhur is delay. An intensive form: He places behind, He defers.
73.aqli: He delays what He wills: a chastisement that might fall, a grace the soul is not yet ready to receive, a victory that would need to be tamed.
73.sufi: The delay you undergo is not a forgetting: it is often a hidden gift. What arrives later arrives riper.
73.part: Accept the delays of life; do not rush upon the unripe fruit.

74.lugha: From w-ʾ-l; awwal is the first in order — but here, with no second that prefigures Him.
74.aqli: Before Him, nothing. No empty time precedes Him: He is before time.
74.sufi: To meditate on al-Awwal is to go back beyond every thinkable origin. Thought draws back, and back, and finds at last a silence: it is there.
74.part: Seek within yourself what was before your stories: bare consciousness, there where you had not yet an image of yourself.

75.lugha: From ʾ-kh-r; ākhir is the last in order — but with no next-to-last that precedes Him.
75.aqli: After Him, nothing. When all has passed — the worlds, the names, the works — He will be, as He was.
75.sufi: Al-Awwal (اَلْأَوَّل) and al-Ākhir are pronounced together: He is at the beginning and at the end. All that unfolds between the two is inscribed within His parenthesis.
75.part: Keep the end in view: not to be frightened by it, but so as not to waste your time along the way.

76.sens_court: Manifest in every sign of the world.
76.lugha: From ẓ-h-r; ẓuhūr is appearance, the becoming-manifest. Ẓāhir, that which shows itself.
76.aqli: All that exists is a sign of Him. Creation is a calligraphy — each line says something of it.
76.sufi: He is not hidden behind the world; He is manifest within the world, to whoever knows how to look. The eye of the heart sees Him everywhere, the eye of flesh misses Him everywhere.
76.part: Learn to see the signs; to read the visible as a language.

77.lugha: From b-ṭ-n; baṭn, the interior, the belly. Bāṭin, that which is within.
77.aqli: He is inward to every thing, without being confused with it. More inward to me than myself, the tradition will say.
77.sufi: Aẓ-Ẓāhir and al-Bāṭin go together: He is manifest and hidden at once — manifest through the signs, hidden by His essence. To seek Him outside and within is the same gesture.
77.part: Cultivate your inwardness. What is interior to you carries more weight than your appearance.

78.sens_court: Who governs each thing with authority.
78.lugha: The same root as al-Walī (اَلْوَلِيّ) (w-l-y), but inflected toward governance: the wālī is the governor of a province.
78.aqli: He governs the entire universe: not an event occurs outside His decree, not a leaf falls without Him.
78.sufi: To recognise al-Wālī is to cease believing that one holds the reins. You do your part; the governing belongs to another.
78.part: Govern first your own kingdom — your body, your tongue, your heart. The rest will be added to you.

79.lugha: A reflexive form of ʿ-l-w; literally the One who exalts Himself above all analogy.
79.aqli: He surpasses not only the creatures, but every description one might form of Him. Tanzīh — transcendence — is the proper sense of this Name.
79.sufi: When you believe you have understood God, recall al-Mutaʿālī: He is beyond what you have understood.
79.part: Rise above the petty: do not waste your time in basenesses, aim higher than life pulls you.

80.sens_court: Of a goodness that runs ahead of the request.
80.lugha: From b-r-r; birr is acting goodness, the doing of good; bārr (بَارّ) qualifies the son devoted to his parents. Al-Barr is goodness in essence.
80.aqli: His goodness does not wait for merit: it is first, free, overflowing.
80.sufi: You are not loved because you deserve it; you deserve because you are loved. This inversion is grace.
80.part: Be good by habit, not by calculation. And be bārr toward those to whom you owe: parents, masters, faithful friends.

81.sens_court: Who returns ceaselessly toward the one who returns toward Him.
81.lugha: From t-w-b; tawba (التَّوْبَة), the return. An intensive form: He returns toward the penitent — even before the penitent returns toward Him.
81.aqli: Human repentance is preceded by a divine movement: He turns His face toward us, and it is this that allows us to turn toward Him.
81.sufi: Every conversion, every spiritual resumption, is a response to an inner call — which is already at-Tawwāb at work.
81.part: Return — each time you stray. And welcome the return of others: do not throw their past in their face.

82.lugha: From n-q-m; often translated as vengeance, but al-Kāfījī specifies: divine intiqām is a restoration, not a passion. He is not wounded; He re-establishes the order.
82.aqli: When injustice is prolonged, al-Muntaqim acts — sometimes slowly, sometimes suddenly. The balance always rights itself.
82.sufi: Do not avenge yourself; that is not your role, it is His. And He does it better than you would.
82.part: Leave to God His function. Keep, for yourself, forgiveness and service.

83.sens_court: Who effaces the fault down to its trace.
83.lugha: From ʿ-f-w; ʿafw (عَفْو) is effacement, the wiping clean. Stronger than maghfira (which veils): ʿafw leaves not even a trace.
83.aqli: The forgiveness of al-Ghafūr (اَلْغَفُور) covers; that of al-ʿAfū effaces. As though the fault had never occurred.
83.sufi: Allāhumma innaka ʿafuwwun karīmun tuḥibbu-l-ʿafwa fa-ʿfu ʿannī — “My God, You are the One who effaces, generous, who loves to efface: efface my faults.” The invocation of the Nights of Destiny.
83.part: Forgive without reminding. Do not bring up another's old faults again: leave them truly behind.

84.lugha: From r-ʾ-f; raʾfa is the compassion that softens, gentler still than raḥma.
84.aqli: If ar-Raḥmān (اَلرَّحْمَٰن) is wide mercy, ar-Raʾūf is the tenderness that bends down — the one that takes into its arms.
84.sufi: When you feel too small for ar-Raḥmān, invoke ar-Raʾūf: the same tenderness, but nearer, more tangible.
84.part: Be tender. The world cruelly lacks tenderness — above all where it claims to have no need of it.

85.sens_court: Master of every kingdom, visible and invisible.
85.lugha: A construction in two words: Mālik, possessor; al-Mulk, the kingdom, sovereignty. Possessor of the Kingdom.
85.aqli: He gives the kingdom to whom He wills, withdraws it from whom He wills: no throne is held for life.
85.sufi: The human being too receives a small kingdom — his body, his time, his gifts. But it is a lent kingdom, for which an account will have to be given.
85.part: Rule your kingdom with justice; remember that it does not belong to you as your own.

86.fr: The Holder of Majesty and Generosity
86.sens_court: In Him, majesty and beauty do not contradict each other — they coincide.
86.lugha: Dhū, possessor of; al-jalāl (جَلَال), the majesty that inspires awe; al-ikrām, the generosity that honours. The Name says: He alone is both the one and the other.
86.aqli: In creatures, strength and gentleness are opposed. In Him, they are wedded: His majesty is generous, His generosity is majestic.
86.sufi: It is the Name of the coincidence of opposites in God — a vertigo dear to the Sufis: neither hard nor soft, neither distant nor cloying, but both in their perfection.
86.part: Cultivate within yourself jalāl (dignity, firmness) and ikrām (gentleness, generosity). The one without the other is disfigured.

87.sens_court: Who renders to each his due.
87.lugha: From q-s-ṭ; qisṭ is equity, the just portion. An active form: the One who balances.
87.aqli: Different from al-ʿAdl (اَلْعَدْل), which is essential Justice: al-Muqsiṭ is equity in act, distributed — He renders to each one what he is due to receive, neither more nor less.
87.sufi: Divine equity often reveals itself beyond appearances. What seems unequal to you today balances within an economy vaster than you see.
87.part: Be equitable in small things: in the apportioning of time, of attention, of speech. No one should be wronged by you.

88.lugha: From j-m-ʿ; jamʿ, the gathering. An active form: the One who gathers.
88.aqli: He will gather humankind on the promised Day; and already He gathers the opposites within each being (life and death, suffering and joy, day and night).
88.sufi: Al-Jāmiʿ also gathers the scattered heart: jamʿ al-qalb (القَلْب) is inner unification, in which a thousand preoccupations give way to a single presence.
88.part: Gather: what within you is scattered, what around you is divided. Do not be an agent of separation.

89.lugha: From gh-n-y; ghinā (غِنَى), richness, sufficiency. Ghanī, the one who has need of nothing.
89.aqli: He is rich in Himself, and we can add nothing to Him by our worship, nor subtract anything from Him by our neglect.
89.sufi: Whether you pray to Him or not, He is no greater for it; but you become more yourself. Worship serves the servant, not the Lord.
89.part: Tend toward inner ghinā: poverty toward creatures and inner richness. That is freedom.

90.sens_court: Who fulfils the needs of His servants.
90.lugha: A causative form of the same root: the One who makes ghanī, who makes sufficient.
90.aqli: He makes His servants rich — of money sometimes, of heart above all. Many are poor with much; a few (الأُنْس) are rich with nothing.
90.sufi: Al-Mughnī enriches the soul until it no longer needs the goods of the world. That is the most solid wealth.
90.part: Help to enrich others — first of all inwardly. The greatest gift is not material.

91.sens_court: Who prevents what would do harm.
91.lugha: From m-n-ʿ; manʿ, to prevent, to forbid. An active form: the One who withholds.
91.aqli: He withholds what He wills: a gift one asks of Him, an evil one feared, an event one hoped for. And His withholding is as much a grace as His giving.
91.sufi: Often, what you did not obtain was your protection. Al-Māniʿ sometimes withholds for your good — you will know it later, perhaps.
91.part: Learn to withhold: your anger, your tongue, your desire to have the last word. Prevention is an act of strength.

92.sens_court: The ultimate source of every trial, which comes only through wisdom.
92.lugha: From ḍ-r-r; ḍarr, harm, injury. A Name to be handled with reverence: it says that nothing can do harm outside His decree.
92.aqli: No affliction comes without His permission. This does not mean that He loves evil — it means that nothing escapes His ordinance.
92.sufi: To understand aḍ-Ḍārr heals one of rancour against the world: what comes to you, even by another's hand, is also by His permission. You no longer have to struggle against the shadows: you turn toward the Source.
92.part: Never be a cause of harm, to the measure of your power; but accept harm undergone as a part of the great economy you do not yet understand.

93.sens_court: The ultimate source of all good.
93.lugha: From n-f-ʿ; nafʿ (نَفْع), benefit, usefulness. An active form: the One who does good.
93.aqli: No good comes without His being its source. The hands that do you good are His hands.
93.sufi: Aḍ-Ḍārr and an-Nāfiʿ are named together: one must hold to both so as not to fall into dualism. Apparent good and evil come from the same Source, within a single wisdom.
93.part: Be useful. Seek the nafʿ in each of your days — what good have you brought, and to whom?

94.sens_court: Light of the heavens and the earth, and light of hearts.
94.lugha: From n-w-r; nūr, light. Not a visual attribute: the light by which something is seen.
94.aqli: Allāh (اَللَّٰه) is the Light of the heavens and the earth (Quran 24:35). He is that by which all becomes manifest: without Him, nothing would be seen, hence nothing would be.
94.sufi: An-Nūr is also the inner light of the heart — knowledge by unveiling (kashf (الكَشْف)). You do not kindle this light: you withdraw the veils, and it is there.
94.part: Be a light, modestly: illumine around you, not too much, just enough that one may see.

95.lugha: From h-d-y; hidāya (هِدَايَة), guidance. An active form: the One who guides in the present.
95.aqli: He guides on several levels: general guidance (the senses, the intelligence), prophetic guidance (the revealed messages), intimate guidance (the inspiration of the heart).
95.sufi: One does not guide oneself to the Real; one is guided. All you can do is make yourself guidable: sincere, attentive, humble.
95.part: Ask for guidance before all the rest. Ihdinā-ṣ-ṣirāṭa-l-mustaqīm — “Guide us upon the straight way”: the prayer one recites seventeen times a day.

96.lugha: From b-d-ʿ; ibdāʿ is to create an entirely new thing, without precedent. Different from khalq (to create from a prior measure).
96.aqli: Before Him, there was nothing from which He could have drawn inspiration; the models, it is He who set them down.
96.sufi: Each day is badīʿ: it has never existed before. Each face you cross is an original. Creation is today, not yesterday.
96.part: Live each day as a new day; and dare, in your life, to create what did not exist before you.

97.lugha: From b-q-y; baqāʾ (بَقَاء), subsistence, permanence. The contrary of fanāʾ (فَنَاء), evanescence.
97.aqli: All passes, save His Face: kullu shayʾin hālik illā wajhah (Quran 28:88). He is what remains when all the rest has come undone.
97.sufi: Fanāʾ fī-Llāh and baqāʾ bi-Llāh — extinction in God and subsistence through God — are the two poles of the way. To die to the transient in order to subsist through the Permanent.
97.part: Place your heart in what does not pass: truth, true love, the good done in secret. These are the only coins one carries away.

98.sens_court: To whom all returns in the end.
98.lugha: From w-r-th; wirth, the inheritance. Wārith, the one who inherits.
98.aqli: When all people will have passed — their goods, their works, their names — He will remain, the sole Inheritor of all.
98.sufi: To meditate on this Name relativises every attachment to possessions: you only occupy temporarily. The true owner comes after you.
98.part: Do not believe yourself the owner of what you hold. You are its usufructuary — and soon the inheritance will return to the Inheritor.

99.lugha: From r-sh-d; rushd is rectitude, maturity, the sense of the right path. Different from hidāya (هِدَايَة): rushd stresses the soundness of the direction taken.
99.aqli: All His ways lead to the good, even those that seem contrary. His direction is never mistaken — ours, sometimes, does not know how to read it.
99.sufi: To ask for rushd is to ask not to take the wrong path once already on the path. Many have guidance and still lose the direction.
99.part: Seek spiritual maturity (rushd) more than worldly wisdom. Worldly wisdom impresses; rushd transforms.

100.lugha: From ṣ-b-r; ṣabr (صَبْر), patience, the holding firm within the trial. A faʿūl (فَعُول) form: He is essentially and magnificently patient.
100.aqli: He defers the chastisement, gives time, awaits the return, begins again with whoever begins again. His patience covers our centuries as it covers our seconds.
100.sufi: Of all the virtues, ṣabr is perhaps the most divine: it is cited in the Quran more than 90 times. Without it, no way holds for long.
100.part: Be patient: with others, with yourself, with God (in appearance) — without ascribing any forgetting to Him. Patience is the other name of trust.
